title: Repo as System of Record for Research
subtitle: A research repo can store plans, sources, assumptions, data definitions, artifacts, and verification records in one reviewable history.
summary: RAIL makes the repository the contract for research plans, source definitions, ontology schemas, hydration pipelines, artifacts, and agent handoffs.

artifact.title: Research state as connected artifacts
artifact.description: Plans, sources, assumptions, tasks, and outputs become connected nodes rather than scattered files.

contentBlocks.text: Research fragments easily: notebooks, dashboards, chats, local CSVs, screenshots, API calls, and half-remembered assumptions. A repo-native system treats those pieces as versioned artifacts with explicit relationships.

contentBlocks.content:
PlanWhat question is being answered?
SourcesWhich data and documents are allowed?
ArtifactsWhat outputs were produced?
VerificationWhat checks make the output trustworthy?

sections.heading: Why Git Helps
body: Git records changes over time. For research, that means assumptions, source definitions, transformations, and results can be reviewed as a history rather than remembered as vibes.
The repo also gives agents a durable workspace: they can resume from files, not from a fading chat transcript.

sections.heading: What Belongs in the Repo
body: Not everything belongs as raw data. Large or sensitive datasets may stay external, but the repo should store definitions, access notes, schemas, checksums, query descriptions, and reproducible scripts.
The goal is not to turn Git into a warehouse. The goal is to make the research contract inspectable.

pitfalls: Committing sensitive raw data by accident.
Letting notebooks become undocumented black boxes.
Using agents without durable handoff records.

quiz.question: What does 'repo as system of record' mean?
options: The repository stores the reviewable research contract and artifacts
Every dataset must be committed raw
quiz.explanation: The repo preserves plans, sources, assumptions, artifacts, and verification context.
